text: The surgeon performs piecemeal dissection of the triangle of Calot, alternating between anterior and posterior views to manage moderate inflammation. Approximately one third of the gallbladder fossa is dissected before clipping or cutting any structures to avoid aberrant anatomy.
The cholangiogram catheter is inserted into the abdomen through a 14 French angiocatheter placed in the epigastric region, visible as a small needle puncture without an incision.

The surgeon places one clip on the distal cystic duct and two clips on the cystic artery. Using hook cautery, a ductotomy is created, followed by the placement of a third arterial clip. The empty clip applier is used to maneuver the cholangiogram catheter into the duct.
The surgeon performs an intraoperative cholangiogram by undocking one robotic arm, followed by placing clips on the proximal cystic duct.
The surgeon transects the cystic duct and cystic artery, followed by the removal of the gallbladder from the fossa using electrocautery.
The surgeon closes the abdominal wall using standard suturing techniques, and the angiocatheter puncture site is sealed with Dermabond or Steristrips.
